artifacts.content:
Hi Mama,

I’m on the train back to Bolzano and finally have a quiet moment. I’ve been thinking again about how to frame my thesis so it really reflects what we went through with Pension Innerhofer, not just some abstract “digitalisation” story.

I keep coming back to those months after Papa’s mess in 2019. When the bank in Brunico first called about the €85,000 in gambling-related debts and the possible near‑bankruptcy of the guesthouse, it felt like the ground disappeared under our feet. I still see you and Papa sitting in the office at Via Stazione 14, going through the numbers and that informal restructuring agreement with the bank, while I tried to figure out how online booking and better pricing tools might help us climb out of the hole.

Part of me wants to write very openly about it, something like “Digitalisation and Resilience of Family‑Run SMEs in South Tyrol: A Case Study of Pension Innerhofer’s Recovery After Papa Markus’ 2019 Gambling Debts and Near‑Bankruptcy in Brunico”. It would show how desperate we were when we started pushing Booking.com, Airbnb, Instagram, the channel manager, all of it. But I also know it’s your and Papa’s story, not just mine.

Don’t worry, I wouldn’t publish anything without changing names and checking with you both first. Maybe I’ll just keep the full version as a kind of private working title and then anonymise everything later.

Anyway, I’ll be home again next weekend to help with check‑ins. Can you please remind Papa not to book any late arrivals after 22:00 on Sunday? I have to be back in Bolzano early Monday for the methods seminar.

Un abbraccio,
Lukas

metadata.subject: Thinking about how honest to be in my thesis

artifacts.content:
Dear Prof. Rossi,

I hope your week is going well. I wanted to update you on how I’m concretely integrating our own family business and my uncle’s craft firm into the empirical part of the thesis.

For Pension Innerhofer, my parents Markus and Helga have given me access to the internal booking.com and Airbnb performance data from March 2020 to September 2022. I’ve already exported monthly occupancy rates, average daily room prices in euros, and the detailed records of cancellations and refunds during the strict COVID‑19 lockdowns from the office computer at Via Stazione 14 in Brunico. It’s sobering to see whole weeks at 0% occupancy next to long lists of refunds.

In parallel, I spoke again with my uncle, Peter Mair, about using anonymised data from his small electrical business in Val Pusteria. He agreed in principle that I can work with his 2020 and 2021 quarterly VAT declarations and information on the overdraft limits on his business account at Raiffeisenkasse Bruneck. The idea is to show how the delayed adoption of cloud‑based invoicing software affected his liquidity and ability to pay suppliers on time during the energy price spike in autumn 2022.

Methodologically, I imagine treating both as in‑depth case studies within the broader survey: one tourism SME and one craft SME, both family‑run, both pushed toward digital tools by crisis situations (lockdowns and energy costs). I’ll of course pseudonymise the firms and individuals and avoid publishing any raw financial figures that could make them identifiable.

If you have time next week, I’d appreciate 20–30 minutes to discuss how far I can go in describing these cases without crossing any ethical or data protection lines.

Best regards,
Lukas Innerhofer

metadata.subject: Integrating Pension Innerhofer and Peter’s firm as case studies

artifacts.content:
Personal reflection – why this topic still feels raw (draft for myself, not for anyone else)

When I think back to April–June 2020, I don’t just remember “digitalisation” in an abstract way. I remember sitting night after night in the living room at Pension Innerhofer with Mama and Papa, laptops open, trying to decode the constantly changing digital contact‑tracing and hygiene reporting rules from the Sanitätsbetrieb in Bozen. Every new PDF felt like a different language.

Then in May 2020 came the call about Oma Rosa: positive COVID‑19 test, immediate transfer and isolation in the retirement wing of Krankenhaus Bruneck. That was the tipping point. Within a week I had hacked together an online pre‑check‑in form and insisted we use it for 100% of arriving guests, just to reduce physical contact at reception. It was clumsy at first, but it became normal.

At the same time, in the background there was still the fallout from Papa’s 2019 gambling debts and the near‑bankruptcy of the guesthouse – the €85,000, the tense meetings at the bank in Brunico, the informal restructuring agreement. We were trying to keep up with digital tracing rules while also convincing the bank that we were a “resilient” family business.

And later, in autumn 2022, I saw a similar pattern with Onkel Peter’s electrical business in Val Pusteria: delayed digital invoicing, quarterly VAT declarations for 2020 and 2021 that showed how thin his margins were, constant calls with Raiffeisenkasse Bruneck about overdraft limits as energy prices spiked and suppliers waited for payments.

All of that is why I can’t treat this thesis as just a neutral exercise. It’s basically our last few years in graph and regression form.

metadata.title: Why this thesis topic still hurts a bit

artifacts.content:
Hey Anna,

How’s Innsbruck treating you this week? Still surviving night shifts and the Mensa coffee?

I was thinking about you a lot yesterday while drafting the “personal motivation” bit for my thesis intro. I almost wrote the full story of summer semester 2022 – how your burnout during nursing school in Innsbruck basically forced me to rethink how we run things at Pension Innerhofer.

I remember those months so clearly: you calling 2–3 times a week on video, totally exhausted after practice at the Krankenhaus, saying you couldn’t sleep and everything felt too much. I was bouncing between Bolzano, Brunico and Innsbruck, trying to free up weekends for trips to see you. That’s when I really started pushing more digital tools at the guesthouse – online check‑in, clearer Booking.com rules, automating replies – so I could spend less time glued to the reception desk and more time on the train to you.

Part of me even played with a crazy thesis title like “Digitalisation as a Coping Strategy for Informal Care Burdens in South Tyrolean Family Businesses: Lessons from Pension Innerhofer While Supporting My Sister Anna Through Her 2022 Burnout in Innsbruck”. Don’t worry, I’m not actually going to use your name like that – it would all be anonymised if I went in that direction.

On top of that, we had Oma Rosa’s depression treatment from 2021–2023, with her 2–3 therapy sessions per month at the psychiatric outpatient clinic at Krankenhaus Bruneck. Every appointment meant re‑organising breakfast shifts and housekeeping with Mama, and I noticed how much easier it was once we relied more on digital booking platforms instead of phone calls.

Anyway, just wanted you to know that a lot of what I’m writing comes from watching you and Oma fight your way through tough times. Text me when you have a free evening – we can do a proper long call.

Bussi,
Lukas

metadata.subject: How you accidentally shaped my thesis

artifacts.content:
Event: Debrief + planning coffee with neighbouring guesthouse owners

Time: 2026-07-18, 09:00–10:30
Location: Café in Bruneck, near Rathaus
Attendees: Lukas (Pension Innerhofer), 3 neighbouring guesthouse owners from Bruneck/Reischach

Notes:

Purpose is to catch up after the crazy last few seasons and to prepare for the next winter. I want to bring printed screenshots from our WhatsApp group chats between 15 March and 30 April 2021 – the ones where we were all panicking about last‑minute cancellations, putting staff on Kurzarbeit, and one of them couldn’t pay the April 2021 INPS contributions on time. Those messages are perfect “critical incidents” to structure later interviews, but for now it’s just to jog everyone’s memory.

I also want to ask how they handled the COVID‑19 quarantine breach incident we had at Pension Innerhofer in March 2021. After the alleged breach by that guest, the inspection from the local Gesundheitsamt in Bruneck, and the threatened €1,200 administrative fine, our online reputation took a real hit. Google and Booking.com were full of comments about us being “irresponsible”. It forced me to learn, overnight, how to respond to negative reviews, communicate hygiene rules clearly on all platforms, and tighten our digital check‑in procedures.

To discuss over coffee:
- What we each changed in our online booking and communication after March 2021.
- Whether anyone else had trouble with health authorities or INPS during that period.
- Ideas for a joint Instagram campaign for Bruneck/Reischach guesthouses next shoulder season.

Bring laptop + charger; might show them some of my preliminary graphs on cancellations and occupancy.

metadata.title: Crisis debrief with Bruneck/Reischach guesthouse owners

artifacts.content:
To‑do list – data + family logistics (Puster Valley weekend)

1. Pension Innerhofer office tasks (Saturday morning)
- Go through Markus’ accounting folders and finish scanning occupancy and cancellation reports from 03/2020–09/2022 for the thesis dataset (booking.com + Airbnb). Need monthly occupancy rates in %, average daily room prices, and detailed cancellation/refund amounts during the strict lockdowns.
- Double‑check that the exported files from the office PC at Via Stazione 14 match what Mama has in the printed binders.

2. Talk with Papa about that old 2020 tax audit
- I still need his memory of the conversation at the Agenzia delle Entrate office in Bolzano when they found approx. €15,000 of previously undeclared income from 2018–2020 (cash‑in‑hand stuff, including some of Onkel Peter’s side jobs).
- Clarify how we ended up paying around €3,500 in back taxes and penalties and why that finally convinced him to switch Pension Innerhofer from cash‑only to proper e‑invoicing and POS systems.
- Ask if he’s okay with me using this as an example of “crisis‑driven digitalisation” (fully anonymised, of course).

3. Call Peter about his business in Val Pusteria
- Check if he’s comfortable with me referencing his delayed move away from paper notebooks and cash‑jobs in the thesis.
- Confirm which parts of his 2020/2021 VAT data and overdraft story with Raiffeisenkasse Bruneck I’m allowed to mention.

4. Family stuff
- Drive Oma Rosa to her psychiatric outpatient appointment at Krankenhaus Bruneck (depression treatment, 2–3 sessions per month).
- Quick visit to Anna on Sunday evening via video call from the kitchen – check how her shifts in Innsbruck are going.
- Help Mama plan breakfast shifts for next week so she doesn’t overdo it again.

metadata.title: Weekend tasks – data, taxes, and family

artifacts.content:
Ciao Peter,

sitting in Bolzano after a long day in the library and finally writing you the follow‑up I promised.

I’ve been sketching out different angles for the “story” parts of my thesis. One idea that keeps coming back is to show how crises pushed both your electrical business in Val Pusteria and Pension Innerhofer to move “from cash‑in‑hand to e‑invoices”. With you it was mainly the energy price spike in autumn 2022 and the constant juggling of supplier payments against the overdraft limits on your account at the Raiffeisenkasse Bruneck. With us it was the 2020 tax audit at the Agenzia delle Entrate in Bolzano, when they uncovered approx. €15,000 of previously undeclared income from 2018–2020 and we ended up paying about €3,500 in back taxes and penalties.

Combined with Papa’s gambling debts in 2019 – the whole €85,000 story and the near‑bankruptcy of the guesthouse before the informal restructuring agreement with the bank in Brunico – it really shows how much pressure there was to finally get serious about digital payment systems, POS, and proper invoicing. Not out of love for technology, but pure survival.

Of course, in the thesis everything would be anonymised: no “Peter Mair”, no “Pension Innerhofer”, no Raiffeisenkasse Bruneck or Agenzia delle Entrate named directly. I’d talk about a “small electrical contractor” and a “family‑run guesthouse” in an Alpine region.

Let me know honestly how you feel about this. If it’s too close to home, I can keep your case more general and focus on the pilot project with the new cloud invoicing app instead.

Saluti dalla città,
Lukas
